Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I was juggling so much with limited resources. I'd usually carve out time in the evenings after my part-time job, often studying at the library since my internet at home isn't always reliable. I focused heavily on reading and writing things out by hand because that's how information sticks for me. I tried to dedicate about 16 hours a week, but those hours were scattered across late nights and early mornings whenever I could grab them.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I was working that week. I couldn't afford the luxury of a consistent schedule—some weeks I'd cram everything into two or three intense sessions, other weeks I'd spread it out more. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. I learned to be flexible because life just doesn't let me have a neat routine.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download materials I could study offline later. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something due and that would prompt me to log in quickly. I also checked in when I was feeling anxious about whether I'd missed something important. It wasn't really a daily habit—more of a functional check-in when I needed specific information.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never fully became routine for me. There were stretches where I'd log in more regularly, especially when assignments were piling up, but it always felt like a tool I used when necessary rather than a habit. My routine was more about my physical notes and downloaded PDFs than the platform itself. The peer support group I joined helped remind me to check in sometimes, which made it slightly more consistent.
Q5: The downloadable readings and lecture notes were absolutely essential for me. Since I learn best through reading and writing, I'd download everything and then work through it on paper, making my own summaries and notes. The assignment guidelines were also really useful because they were clear about what was expected. I appreciated anything I could take offline and study without needing constant internet access.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole course. It's not that I didn't value other perspectives, but I felt self-conscious and didn't have the time or energy to craft thoughtful posts. I also didn't watch many videos all the way through on the platform; I found them less efficient than reading. When you're stretched thin, you prioritize what gives you the most return on your time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand difficult concepts. I needed to see a clear payoff because my time is precious. If something felt like busywork or repetition of what I already knew, I'd skip it. The peer interactions I did have were valuable because they helped clarify confusing topics quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones got my attention because they affected my grade, and I couldn't risk falling behind. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice' but then reality would hit—I had work, I had assignments due, I was tired. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture notes and readings, then print them at the library or work through them on my tablet without internet. I filled notebooks with handwritten summaries and practice answers. I also used some free textbooks I found online and YouTube videos to supplement when I was confused. The LMS was really just my starting point, not where the actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours rewriting my notes, testing myself with flashcards I'd made, or working through practice problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also meet with a couple of classmates from my peer support group in person to talk through difficult concepts. My best studying was invisible to the LMS because it happened in my notebook and in my head.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF or watch the first few minutes of a video to understand what it covered, then I'd work with that material offline for days. I couldn't afford to keep streaming videos or staying connected, so I became really good at grabbing what I needed in one session and then studying it independently. One download might fuel a week's worth of studying.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when everything felt overwhelming. I was working extra shifts because I needed the money, and I was exhausted all the time. I felt sad and disconnected from the course, like I was just barely keeping my head above water. My attendance dropped because I physically couldn't make it to everything. The peer support helped me push through, but those weeks were really hard.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in just enough to grab what I needed and then disappear. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly—sometimes the course lost out to more immediate concerns. Confusion also slowed me down; when I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it until I had the mental energy to tackle it.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials or have a reading open but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be worrying about bills or just too tired to actually absorb anything. My eyes would move across the page but nothing was sticking. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I kept trying because I knew I had to keep pushing forward somehow.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts because they felt intimidating and time-consuming for something that didn't heavily impact my grade. I also avoided some of the video content because watching felt passive and inefficient compared to reading. Sometimes I'd put off starting assignments because the anxiety of not doing well enough was paralyzing. I knew they were important, but my brain would find reasons to delay.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd check the LMS specifically to see what was due and when, then plan my limited study time around those dates. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged with certain materials at all. It's not ideal, but when you're managing so much, deadlines create the structure that my life otherwise lacks.
Q17: Feedback really mattered to me because it told me whether my strategies were working. When I got good marks on assignments, it motivated me to keep going despite everything. Negative feedback was harder—it sometimes made me anxious and avoidant for a few days. But ultimately, knowing where I stood helped me adjust and focus my limited time on what needed improvement.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag. Having clear deadlines helped me plan, but the expectation of regular attendance was really difficult given my work schedule. I appreciated that materials were available to download, which gave me flexibility. But some aspects assumed students had more time and resources than I actually had, which made participation feel like an uphill battle.
Q19: The downloadable resources made engagement much easier—I couldn't have managed without them. But the emphasis on discussion participation was harder for me because I didn't have time to craft thoughtful responses. The video content being so central was also challenging since I learn better through reading. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate engagement would have helped students like me.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it, so they keep clicking around hoping something will click. Or they might be dealing with anxiety or confusion that keeps them coming back but unable to actually complete work. Life circumstances could also mean they have time to browse but not the focused energy to produce assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low, but I performed decently because my real learning happened offline. I'd grab materials quickly and then spend hours with my handwritten notes away from the computer. Someone looking at my login data would think I wasn't trying, but I was working really hard—just not in ways the system could see. Efficiency matters when your time is limited.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in, which gives a basic picture of interaction patterns. But they completely miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they read, how much time they spent thinking about concepts offline, or what personal struggles affected their participation. The numbers tell you what happened on the platform, not what happened in someone's mind or life.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions even if just in my own head, and connecting new concepts to what I already know. It's about caring enough to push through when things get hard. For me, engagement often looks like filling pages of notes and losing track of time while working through a difficult concept. It's an internal state that doesn't always have external markers.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent at the library with printed notes, the peer study sessions that happened in person, or the mental effort I put into understanding concepts while riding the bus to work. It wouldn't capture the sadness I pushed through or the resilience it took to keep going despite financial stress. My engagement was real and deep, but it happened in spaces the system couldn't reach.
